narrative.springFall: May through June and September are Geneva's signature seasons. Temperatures sit in a comfortable 18–24°C, the lake warms to swimmable, Mont Blanc stays clear, and café terraces along the quais fill late into the evening. Vineyards in Mandement are pruned and green in spring, gold and harvest-busy in September. If you're choosing your first visit, aim for late May or mid-September—long light, fewer crowds, and the whole calendar of Alpine day trips at peak condition.
narrative.summer: July and August bring the longest, brightest days—10pm sunsets, swimming at Bains des Pâquis and Genève-Plage, Fêtes de Genève fireworks over the harbour, and Musiques en été free open-air concerts. The Léman Express runs full schedules to Alpine trailheads. Trade-offs: peak hotel rates around the major UN agendas, August 1 (Swiss National Day) closures, and many Genevan locals decamping to their chalets. Book lakefront tables and cable-car rides in advance.
narrative.winter: December through February is fondue season—warm bistros, raclette stations, glüwein at the Noël au Quai market, and the Escalade festival (mid-December) reliving Geneva's 1602 victory. Mont Blanc and the Jura sit a 90-minute train from Cornavin, so the city doubles as a base for Chamonix, Verbier, and Megève skiing. Temperatures hover at 0–6°C, fog can shroud the basin for days—but sunshine above 700m is reliably brilliant. Hotel rates drop outside ski-Saturday peaks.
narrative.march: Geneva's gentle thaw. Temperatures climb toward 10°C, the first crocus and daffodils appear at the Jardin Anglais and Parc des Bastions, and lakefront walks return without down jackets. The bise wind can still bite—pack a windproof shell. Shoulder-season value is excellent: hotel rates ease, restaurant bookings open up, and you can still pair a city stay with a final spring-ski day in the Portes du Soleil.
